case_name: Eichmann Trial (Attorney General of Israel v Adolf Eichmann)
court: District Court of Jerusalem / Supreme Court of Israel
citation: Attorney General of Israel v Adolf Eichmann, Criminal Case No. 40/61 (District Court of Jerusalem, 1961); Criminal Appeal No. 336/61 (Supreme Court of Israel, 1962)
text: EICHMANN TRIAL (1961-1962). COURTS: District Court of Jerusalem (1961); Supreme Court of Israel (1962). FACTS: Adolf Eichmann, a former senior Nazi SS officer who played a central role in organizing the deportation of Jews to concentration and extermination camps during the Holocaust, was captured by Israeli agents in Argentina in 1960 and brought to Israel. He was charged under the Nazis and Nazi Collaborators (Punishment) Law, 5710-1950, with crimes against the Jewish people, crimes against humanity, and war crimes. Eichmann raised objections regarding jurisdiction, arguing that Israel did not exist at the time of the crimes, that the crimes were not committed in Israeli territory, and that the manner of his capture (abduction from Argentina) violated international law. HOLDING: The District Court convicted Eichmann on all counts and sentenced him to death. The Supreme Court upheld the conviction and sentence. On the jurisdictional question, the courts held that Israel had jurisdiction based on the UNIVERSAL JURISDICTION principle for crimes of such magnitude, as well as the PASSIVE PERSONALITY principle (the victims being Jewish persons who would later constitute the Israeli nation). REASONING: The Court held that crimes against the Jewish people, crimes against humanity, and war crimes are international crimes subject to universal jurisdiction. Every state has the right to punish such crimes, regardless of where they occurred or the nationality of the perpetrators or victims. The manner of Eichmann's capture did not deprive Israel of jurisdiction. SIGNIFICANCE: (1) Landmark application of UNIVERSAL JURISDICTION for international crimes. (2) Key precedent in INTERNATIONAL CRIMINAL LAW and the prosecution of crimes against humanity. (3) Foundational in establishing Israel's unique jurisdictional claim based on the connection between the Jewish people and the State of Israel. (4) Influenced the development of the principle that the manner of obtaining custody does not vitiate jurisdiction (male captus, bene detentus).

case_name: Adalah v Minister of Interior (Citizenship and Entry into Israel Law)
citation: HCJ 7052/03, Adalah v Minister of Interior (2006)
text: ADALAH v MINISTER OF INTERIOR (2006). COURT: Supreme Court of Israel (sitting as the High Court of Justice). FACTS: The Citizenship and Entry into Israel Law (Temporary Order) 2003 imposed a blanket prohibition on the granting of residency or citizenship in Israel to Palestinians from the West Bank and Gaza Strip who married Israeli citizens, primarily affecting Arab citizens of Israel. Adalah (The Legal Center for Arab Minority Rights in Israel) and other organizations challenged the law as violating the right to family life, equality, and dignity. HOLDING: The Supreme Court upheld the law by a narrow 6-5 majority, finding that it served the legitimate purpose of security within the context of the ongoing Israeli-Palestinian conflict, and that the proportionality test was satisfied. REASONING: The majority held that while the law infringed on the constitutional right to family life, the infringement was proportionate to the security threat posed by the entry of Palestinians into Israel during the Second Intifada. The minority (including Chief Justice Barak) argued that the blanket prohibition was disproportionate and that individual security assessments should be conducted. SIGNIFICANCE: (1) Key case on the tension between SECURITY and HUMAN RIGHTS in Israeli law. (2) Important precedent on the PROPORTIONALITY test in Israeli constitutional law. (3) Significant in the debate over citizenship and immigration rights in conflict zones.

case_name: Beit Sourik Village Council v Government of Israel (Separation Barrier Case)
citation: HCJ 2056/04, Beit Sourik Village Council v Government of Israel (2004)
text: BEIT SOURIK VILLAGE COUNCIL v GOVERNMENT OF ISRAEL (2004). COURT: Supreme Court of Israel (High Court of Justice). FACTS: Palestinian villages challenged the route of the separation barrier/fence constructed by Israel in the West Bank, arguing that it did not follow the Green Line but extended deep into occupied territory, separating Palestinians from their agricultural land and imposing disproportionate hardship. HOLDING: Chief Justice Aharon Barak, writing for the Court, held that while Israel had the authority to construct a separation barrier for security purposes, the specific route of the barrier in the area at issue was unlawful because it was disproportionate — the injury to the local inhabitants was not proportionate to the security benefit gained. The Court ordered the rerouting of approximately 30 km of the barrier. REASONING: The Court applied the THREE-PART PROPORTIONALITY TEST: (1) the means chosen must be rationally connected to the objective; (2) the means must cause the least possible injury to the individual; (3) the injury must be proportionate to the benefit gained. The Court found that the specific route failed the third element of proportionality. The Court applied international humanitarian law (Hague Regulations, Fourth Geneva Convention) and held that the military commander must balance security needs with the rights and welfare of the protected population. SIGNIFICANCE: (1) Leading application of PROPORTIONALITY analysis in security contexts. (2) Important precedent on the justiciability of military operations during occupation. (3) One of the most significant judicial interventions in the Israeli-Palestinian conflict.
